attributes.stem:
An 80-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner with a two day history of lower abdominal pain and constipation. She has no other digestive tract symptoms. Her mobility is limited because of shortness of breath related to chronic obstructive airways disease. Her history also includes ischaemic heart disease and hypertension. Her current medications are aspirin and perindopril. She is a reformed smoker and drinks a glass of wine most days.

On examination her blood pressure is 138/76 mmHg, pulse 90/min, temperature 37.2oC, respiratory rate 15/min and SpO2 96% on room air. Abdominal examination is unremarkable apart from some mild tenderness in the left iliac fossa. Routine bloods and urinalysis are performed and whilst results of these are awaited the practitioner takes the precaution of arranging a CT scan (Image). The laboratory studies come back normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: With regards to the CT findings, which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
From the history the patient may have had an episode of diverticulitis. A CT scan would be a reasonable investigation to perform to confirm such a diagnosis. In this case no abnormalities have been found that might explain her current symptoms. However, there is an incidental finding of a calcified gallbladder (also known as porcelain gallbladder). The calcification extends around the rim of the gallbladder.

Historically it was considered by physicians that as porcelain gallbladder was associated with an increased risk of gallbladder cancer that it should be treated with open cholecystectomy, however recent research has proved that even though the risk of gallbladder cancers is increased with porcelain gallbladder, the risk is very slight and not sufficient to warrant an open operative procedure to remove the gallbladder. Therefore there is no current consensus on the operative approach, however laparoscopic cholecystectomy is considered the treatment of choice if there are gallbladder symptoms present indicating cholelithiasis or for asymptomatic patients who are fit for surgery.

Management can also be guided by the pattern of calcification. While historically all porcelain gallbladders were thought to carry an increased risk of malignancy, recent evidence suggests that the risk varies with the pattern of calcification. Where there is selective mucosal calcification, there is a higher risk of malignancy than complete intramural calcification. For an otherwise healthy patient with selective mucosal calcification and without significant comorbidities, prophylactic removal is recommended to eliminate this risk. This particular patient has an almost circumferential margin of calcification and does not need to be offered surgery,

For the asymptomatic patient with co-morbidities or a limited life expectancy, as in this case, a conservative approach would be prudent. The CT finding should be explained to her and she should be reassured that the risk of malignant change is very slight and the risks associated surgery would be considerable. Given her general state of health, even if there was selective mucosal calcification and an increased risk of gallbladder malignancy, it is unlikely she would be offered surgery.

Even if the risk of gallbladder malignancy is increased with mucosal calcifications, rather than transmural calcification, it is unlikely that further imaging is going to be of any value and an MRI scan is unlikely to add anything that is not already evident.

This patient could be offered regular follow up with imaging, but ultrasonography would provide all the information that would be sought on further imaging and yearly follow up with CT would be unnecessary.

Open cholecystectomy was traditionally used as a treatment option in patients with calcified gallbladder with gallbladder symptoms or calcified gallbladder without gallbladder symptoms because of the belief that a calcified gallbladder is too "brittle" to be taken out from a laparoscopic port, but with new studies, this belief has been rejected and now open cholecystectomy is only indicated in patients with gallbladder indications of open cholecystectomy, such as dense adhesions.
learningPoints: A patient with an incidental finding of porcelain gallbladder should be advised for a yearly follow-up with an ultrasound.
references: Khan ZS, Livingston EH, Huerta S. Reassessing the need for prophylactic surgery in patients with porcelain gallbladder: case series and systematic review of the literature. Arch Surg. 2011;146(10):1143–1147. doi: 10.1001/archsurg.2011.257. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1001/archsurg.2011.257

DesJardins H, Huy L, Scheirey C, et al. Porcelain gallbladder: is observation a safe option in select populations? Am Coll Surg. 2018. Available at: https://www.facs.org/for-medical-professionals/news-publications/news-and-articles/jacs/

Chen GL, Akmal Y, DiFronzo AL, et al. Porcelain gallbladder: no longer an indication for prophylactic cholecystectomy. Am Surg. 2018;81:936–940. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1177/000313481808400908
